---
date: "2026-06-22"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
---

## Faking the Universe — the Sampling Distribution

- Part 10 — seeing the sampling distribution by simulation

::: notes
Welcome back to Chapter one-point-three, part ten of eleven. In part nine we drew two warnings about consistency. We have computed two summaries of the sampling distribution — its center and its spread — but never seen the object itself. In this part we name it, then fake the universe on a computer: we redraw the sample thousands of times and watch the summaries we proved on paper appear in front of us.
:::

## Name the Object

- **Sampling distribution**: distribution of estimates over all samples of size $N$
- Priya draws once: one sample, one $\bar{x}=8410$
- In simulation we are gods: redraw 10,000 times
- Each redraw: 200 fresh claims, compute $\bar{x}$, record
- The 10,000 $\bar{x}$ values *are* the sampling distribution

::: notes
We have been talking about the distribution of x bar across all possible samples as if it were a real object. It is, and it has a name: the sampling distribution of an estimator is the probability distribution of the estimates you would get across all possible samples of a given size N from the population. Here is the conceptual core of the whole chapter. In real life Priya gets one sample of two hundred claims, and therefore one estimate, x bar equals eight thousand four hundred ten. She can never see the sampling distribution, because she only ever draws once. But in a simulation we are gods: we know the true population, so we can draw a fresh sample of two hundred, compute its x bar, write it down, throw the sample away, and repeat ten thousand times. The ten thousand x bar values we collect are a picture of the sampling distribution. We are watching the random variable x bar do its thing over and over, instead of seeing it just once.
:::

## Build It, and Check the Summaries

- Model claims as **lognormal**, mean $\mu=\$8{,}000$
- Right-skewed: many small claims, a thin catastrophe tail
- Draw 200, average; repeat 10,000 times
- Mean of the 10,000 $\bar{x}$ lands on $\$8{,}000$ — unbiased, *seen*
- Their std dev matches $\sigma/\sqrt{N}$ — the standard error

::: notes
Let us build it. We model individual claims with a right-skewed distribution — a lognormal, the standard rough model for insurance losses, many small claims and a thin tail of catastrophes — calibrated so the population mean is eight thousand dollars. We write a function that draws two hundred claims, averages them, and repeats ten thousand times, collecting the means. Two lines of the output deserve a standing ovation. First, the mean of the ten thousand x bar values lands essentially on eight thousand dollars: the sampling distribution is centered on mu, exactly as the unbiasedness proof promised — now seen, not just derived. Second, the standard deviation of those ten thousand estimates matches sigma over the square root of N from part four. That standard deviation of the sampling distribution has its own famous name, the standard error, and the simulation shows you literally what it measures: the typical sample-to-sample wobble of your estimate. The formula and the histogram are two views of the same object.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The **sampling distribution** is the distribution of estimates across all samples of size $N$ — invisible to Priya, who draws once, but buildable by redrawing 10,000 times in simulation. The mean of those estimates confirms unbiasedness; their spread confirms the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{N}$. Next: the *shape*, and how it changes with $N$.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part ten: the sampling distribution is the distribution of estimates across all possible samples of a given size. Priya can never see it, because she draws only once, but in simulation we redraw ten thousand times and the cloud of estimates is the sampling distribution made visible. Two facts we proved on paper now appear in the output: the mean of the ten thousand estimates lands on the true mean, confirming unbiasedness, and their standard deviation matches sigma over the square root of N, the standard error. Formula and histogram are two views of one object. But we have only confirmed the center and the spread. In part eleven, the finale, we look at the part the formulas cannot give us — the shape of the distribution, and how it transforms as the sample grows.
:::
